citation.title: Selective lithium recovery using bacterial cellulose acetate membranes: toward green recycling of spent Li-ion batteries

content: A B S T R A C T The global transition to electric vehicles and renewable energy systems has heightened the demand for lithiumion batteries (LIBs), creating an urgent need for sustainable battery recycling methods to recover critical raw materials, including lithium. Lithium-selective cation-exchange polymeric membranes are one of the emerging options to achieve such lithium recycling. To make this change even greener, instead of using traditional fossilorigin polymers to produce membranes, this research employed bacterial cellulose acetate (BCA), a bio-derived and eco-friendly polymer. By adding 5 wt% N-methyl-N-propylpiperidinium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (PP13–TFSI), an ionic liquid (IL) which is a plasticizer and lithium-ion conductor, and 20 wt% hydrogen man­ganese oxide (HMO), which is a lithium-selective inorganic filler, four BCA-based membranes (BCA, BCA-IL, BCA-HMO and BCA-IL-HMO) were prepared. The membranes were extensively characterized for their morphology, thermal stability, chemical, and mechanical properties. Subsequently, they were tested in diffusion cells (without applying any external driving force) for ionic conductivity, lithium selectivity, and lithium flux using binary salt mixtures and synthetic LIB leachate.

citation.title: Selective lithium recovery using bacterial cellulose acetate membranes: toward green recycling of spent Li-ion batteries

content: lying any external driving force) for ionic conductivity, lithium selectivity, and lithium flux using binary salt mixtures and synthetic LIB leachate. The BCA-IL membrane outperformed other BCA-based membranes in terms of separation factors, achieving values of 10.50 (Li+/Mn2+), 11.75 (Li+/Ni2+), and 10.95 (Li+/Co2+) with a lithium flux of 0.12 mol m-2 h-1 when processing synthetic LIB leachate. Under the same conditions, the BCA-HMO membranes exhibited a higher lithium flux (0.51 mol m-2 h-1) but with lower sep­aration factor values of 3.39 (Li+/Mn2+), 3.62 (Li+/Ni2+), and 3.36 (Li+/Co2+). The use of plant-derived cel­lulose acetate (CA) as an alternative to BCA was also assessed; however, despite promising ideal lithium selectivity values (for example, 112 for Li+/Ni2+ in the case of CA-HMO membrane), their conductivity was up to two orders of magnitude lower than that of BCA-based membranes. All these findings highlight the promising potential of BCA-based membranes for lithium recovery from lithium-ion battery leachates.

citation.title: Selective lithium recovery using bacterial cellulose acetate membranes: toward green recycling of spent Li-ion batteries

content:
1. Introduction The use of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has increased significantly due to the rapid growth of the electric vehicle and portable electronic de­vices markets [1,2]. At the end of their operational lifespan, spent LIBs are classified as hazardous waste. Improper disposal can lead to the leaching of heavy metals, the release of toxic gases, and fire risks [3,4]. The components of Li-ion battery cathodes, such as lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese, are classified as critical raw materials, and Europe is heavily reliant on their imports [3–5]. Therefore, the efficient recycling of spent LIBs is vital for reducing environmental risks and promoting the development of electric mobility towards a carbon-neutral future. Traditional battery recycling methods face several challenges. Py­rometallurgical processes (high-temperature smelting) are energyintensive, burn fossil fuels, and release substantial amounts of fluoride fumes and carbon dioxide, while losing lithium to the slag phase, which remains unrecovered [6,7]. Alternatively, hydrometallurgical processes, although they use relatively less energy, consume large quantities of chemicals during operations such as acid leaching and precipitation, and

For example, a typical hydrometallurgical LIB recycling plant may generate about 800 kg of sodium sulphate per 1000 kg of batteries processed [9]. Hy­drometallurgy is also not very effective at recovering lithium, which is typically done in the final precipitation step, resulting in significant losses in previous stages and often producing lithium as low-purity salts that require further refining. Currently, only a small fraction (~5 %) of spent batteries is collected and recycled globally using fully developed conventional recycling methods [10,11], highlighting the need for alternative systems that can selectively, efficiently, and sustainably recover lithium. Ion exchange membranes [12–14] are gaining attention as an effi­cient separator to selectively extract lithium from complex LIB leachates via electrochemical or diffusion-driven processes, thus eliminating the need for energy-intensive thermal treatments or chemical intensive processes utilized in traditional systems. content: esses, thus eliminating the need for energy-intensive thermal treatments or chemical intensive processes utilized in traditional systems. Huynh et al. [15] fabricated a polyelectrolyte-based membrane utilizing poly(allylamine hydrochlo­ride) (PAH) and polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) with a lithium/cobalt selectivity of around 12.9 and a lithium flux of 0.068 mol m-2 h-1 at an applied voltage of 1.2 V. Similarly, Li-ion selective polymer inclusion membranes (PIMs) composed of polymer matrix, target ion binder, and plasticizer have also been reported to recover lithium from spent lithium-ion batteries [16]. Wang et al. [17] developed a poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP) based PIM and used it in electrodialysis to separate cobalt from a cobalt-lithium mixture and recovered 74.5 % of cobalt in 24 h at 1.2 V. However, about 5 % of the lithium also migrated along with cobalt, reducing its purity. Adigun et al. [18] also used PVDF-HFP and incorporated Cyanex 301 (target ion binder for Co2+ and Ni2+), and [C8mim][NTf2] (an ionic liquid), to selectively transport Co2+ and Ni2+ from a mixture of Li+, Co2+and Ni2+, achieving 90 % recovery of Co2+ and 73 % of Ni2+. Although many studies have examined the recovery of individual ions from binary mixtures, there is a clear need for a comprehensive study that focuses on the selective recovery of lithium amidst all the cations (Li+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+) typically found in lithium-ion battery leachate.

content: tudy that focuses on the selective recovery of lithium amidst all the cations (Li+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+) typically found in lithium-ion battery leachate. Regarding the use of commercial monoselective ion exchange membranes such as Neo­septa CMS (a sulfonated hydrocarbon polymer), a lithium flux of about 30.0 mol m-2 h-1 and a selectivity of Li+/Mn2+ = 5.4, Li+/Ni2+ = 6.1 and Li+/Co2+ = 5.6 were achieved at 3.0 V [19]. Ion exchange membranes used so far in the lithium battery recycling process, both laboratory-fabricated and commercial, consist of petroleum-derived polymer materials such as poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), sulfonated polyether ether ketone (SPEEK), and sulfonated hydrocarbon-based matrices [16,17,19–21]. These materials present sustainability and environmental concerns due to their fossil origins and non-biodegradability. In particular, PVDF belongs to the polyand perfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), which are persistent and environ­mentally hazardous [22–25]. The REACH framework is being advanced by European regulatory agencies to impose broad restrictions on PFAS-containing compounds, which could significantly limit the future use of fluoropolymer membranes in environmental and industrial ap­plications [24–26]. To replace polymers of fossil origin with more sustainable options, bacterial cellulose (BC) was utilized for the first time in this study to create lithium-selective membranes.

citation.title: Selective lithium recovery using bacterial cellulose acetate membranes: toward green recycling of spent Li-ion batteries

content: il origin with more sustainable options, bacterial cellulose (BC) was utilized for the first time in this study to create lithium-selective membranes. The selection of BC was driven by its high purity, unique nanofibrous structure, and high crystallinity, making it a promising material that can be customized and utilized in various membrane applications [27–30]. In addition to its environ­mental benefits, the production of BC is relatively simpler than cellulose derived from plants (which needs to be separated from lignin and hemicellulose) [31]. Bacterial cellulose can be produced through mi­crobial fermentation using a large variety of agricultural and industrial waste, making it cost-effective and fully integrated into the circular economy [32]. In the present work, BC was produced and then chemically acety­lated to obtain bacterial cellulose acetate (BCA), which served as a polymer base to prepare membranes. For comparison, a commercial cellulose acetate (CA) membrane, derived from plant origin, was also used for the same purpose.

To promote the selectivity of such bio-based membranes for lithium, PP13-TFSI (1-Butyl-1-methylpyrrolidinium bis (trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide), an ionic liquid (IL) that functions as a sustainable plasticizer and lithium ion conductor [33–35], and hydrogen manganese oxide (HMO), a lithium-selective ion sieve, that allows the transport of Li+ ions through its spinal structure [36–39], were incor­porated at different amounts during membrane preparation. The per­formance of membranes was assessed in terms of lithium ideal selectivity and binary separation factors through diffusion studies using single solutions, binary mixtures and synthetic LIB leachate containing Li+, Ni2+, Co2+ and Mn2+. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Bacterial cellulose (BC) biosynthesis Bacterial cellulose was synthesised by cultivating Komagataeibacter sp. NFX107 in Hestrin–Schramm (HS) medium (5 g/L yeast extract, 5 g/ L peptone, 2.7 g/L Na2HPO4, 1.15 g/L citric acid, pH 6), supplemented with 20 g/L glycerol and 3.0 % (v/v) ethanol under static conditions. The colonies that were grown on HS agar plates at a temperature of 28 ◦C for seven days were suspended in liquid HS medium, standardized to an optical density of OD600 = 0.5, and then utilized to produce BC.

Bacterial cellulose was produced by using 200 mL of HS glycerol media in Schott 2.0 L flasks for a period of one month at a temperature of 25 ◦C. A hydrogel-like bacterial cellulose was produced and additionally treated with 0.1 M NaOH at 42 ◦C overnight to eliminate bacterial res­idues, followed by thorough rinsing with deionized water. In the final step, the gel BC was freeze-dried and manually crushed into a powder. The production, purification and storage of BC are comprehensively detailed in our prior study [40]. 2.2. Bacterial cellulose (BC) acetylation Pure BC exhibits high crystallinity and a strong hydrogen bonding network, which hinders its dissolution in traditional organic solvents used for membrane casting [41–43]. The acetylation of bacterial cellu­lose (BC) started by drying the BC powder at 40 ◦C for 2 h. The dried BC (1.5 g) was dispersed in 35 mL of glacial acetic acid and stirred at 50–55 ◦C for 1 h. A catalyst mixture of 0.4 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid and 10 mL of acetic anhydride was prepared separately and added dropwise to the dispersed BC solution at room temperature over 24 h with stirring. After acetylation, the polymer was precipitated in distilled water. The acetylated BC underwent vacuum filtration, washing with distilled water until the pH stabilised (pH = 7) and then drying at 40 ◦C.

The dried material was manually crushed for membrane fabrication. 2.3. Preparation of BCA and CA membranes The BCA and CA membranes were fabricated via a solvent evapo­ration method, by dissolving 5.0 wt% (0.2 g) of dried BCA (or plant origin CA, 9.7 wt% acetyl, average Mn 50,000,Sigma Aldrich) in 4 mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.9 % purity, BP 66 ◦C) at room temperature, with continuous stirring for 24 h to ensure complete dissolution. The prepared homogeneous solution was poured into a glass Petri dish, and a glass funnel was placed on top of the Petri dish to slow down the solvent evaporation to obtain a uniform membrane. The casted membrane was dried for 24 h at room temperature. To obtain lithium-selective membranes, either 0.01 g of 1-Butyl-1methylpyrrolidinium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide (PP13-TFSI, C11H20F6N2O4S2, 98 %, Sigma Aldrich) ionic liquid or 0.04 g of hydrogen manganese oxide (HMO) (synthesised in our laboratory [39]), or both, corresponding to 5 and 20 wt% relative to the polymer mass, respectively, were added into 4 ml of 5 wt% BCA (or CA) solution in THF

A thermogravimetric ana­lyser (TA, Labsys EVO, Setaram, France) was used to ascertain the degradation temperature of the produced membranes, using a heating rate of 10 ◦C.min-1 throughout the temperature range of 33–600 ◦C under a nitrogen environment at a flow rate of 45 mL min-1. The sample weight was approximately 10.0 mg, positioned in an aluminium sample pan. The thermal behaviour of all pure and composite membranes was analysed under an inert environment at 600 ◦C using TGA curves. 2.4.6. Membranes’ ion transport properties 2.4.6.1. Ion exchange capacity. The acid-base titration method [38] was used to assess the IEC of the synthesised membranes. Each membrane sample (0.1 g) was thoroughly rinsed with deionized water and subse­quently air-dried at ambient temperature for 24 h. These samples were then immersed in 100 mL of 0.5 M HCl to ensure that all the fixed components were fully saturated with H+ ions. Afterwards, the samples were washed multiple times until the pH of the washing water was equivalent to that of the deionized water. Then, the membranes were left to air dry before being immersed in 40 mL of 2 M LiCl at room tem­perature for 24 h. Subsequently, they were immersed in a fresh 2.0 M LiCl solution for an additional 24 h to facilitate the release of any bound H+ ions.

The lithium ideal selectivity of mem­branes against manganese, nickel, and cobalt was determined by current-voltage (I–V) curves acquired by linear sweep voltammetry with a Vertex 5A potentiostat (Ivium Technologies, The Netherlands). The membrane under study was positioned between the receiver and feed compartments of the diffusion cell, which both containing a 0.25 M aqueous solution of Li2SO4, MnSO4, CoSO4, or NiSO4. A four-electrode setup was employed in this study (Fig. S2 in the Supporting Informa­tion). A potential difference between -0.2 and + 0.2 V was applied at a scan rate of 10 mV/s, with a step size of 10 mV. Before the measure­ments, the membranes were immersed in the corresponding salt solution for 24 h. The slope of the linear segment of the I–V curves corresponds to the membrane’s conductance (G) (S) in the salt solution in which it is immersed. Conductivity (σ) (S/cm) was calculated using Eq. content: of membranes in recovering lithium from a multi-ionic solution, a synthetic lithium-ion battery leachate solution at pH = 5.8 was prepared (Table 1). After the hydrometallurgical process at which sulfuric acid is used, the pH of the leachate typically starts at 1–2 and is progressively increased to eliminate impurities such as Fe and Al (within a pH range of ~3–5) and then recover Mn at a pH range of ~7–8, Co and Ni at a pH range of ~8–10, and finally, lithium at pH above ~11–13, which can lead to 40 % of its cumulative loss [46]. Thus, the composi­tion and pH at which the membranes were tested correspond to those of the leachate after impurities have been removed. Diffusion tests were conducted in the same two-compartment diffu­sion cell, as previously described in section 2.4.6.4, without applying any external electric potential difference. The feed compartment was filled with the synthetic LIB leachate, while the receiver compartment contained deionized water. The diffusion experiment was carried out for 5 h at room temperature with the tested membrane clamped between the compartments. Ionic concentrations were measured using induc­tively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) in receiver samples (4.0 mL) collected at 0, 0.5, 1, 3, and 5 h. Separation factors and molar ionic fluxes were determined using Eqs. (7) and (8). 3. Results and discussion 3.1.

Membranes morphology Fig. 3 shows the surface morphology and cross-sectional SEM images of all the synthesised membranes. The morphologies of pristine BCA and CA membranes are similar, both appearing relatively smooth and ho­mogeneous, with compact and dense cross-sections. The addition of ionic liquid (IL, PP13–TFSI) slightly altered the morphologies of both CA-IL and BCA-IL membranes, resulting in smoother surfaces. Addi­tionally, their cross-sectional images reveal segmented structures, indi­cating that IL reorganised the polymer matrix. Incorporating the inorganic lithium-selective filler HMO into the membrane formulations resulted in more noticeable structural differences. In the CA-HMO membrane, particles are more evenly distributed within the dense ma­trix and display a rougher surface, whereas the BCA-HMO membrane features a more interconnected filler network within its less dense structure. The embedded HMO filler particles within the polymer matrix are not visible in their cross-sectional images and are more pronounced on the surface. The combination of IL and HMO in BCA-IL-HMO and CAIL-HMO membranes resulted in the roughest, granular surfaces compared to other membranes.

Journal of Membrane Science 737 (2026) 124776 a slight decrease in TS in both polymer membrane types (CA and BCA), corresponds with the plasticizing behaviour of TFSI-based ionic liquids, leading to disruption of intermolecular hydrogen bonding between polymer chains and enhancing segmental chain mobility [55]. Never­theless, this plasticization may improve ion transport (section 3.6). The incorporation of HMO into both BCA and CA also decreased the TS, yet at different magnitudes. The tensile strength in BCA-HMO decreased to 67 ± 20.0 MPa, whereas CA-HMO maintained a compar­atively higher TS of 89 ± 3.3 MPa. This reduction may signify the mismatch at the inorganic-organic interface. In mixed matrix mem­branes, mechanical strength increases only when fillers are uniformly dispersed and strongly bound to the polymer matrix [56]. The most substantial decline in mechanical strength was observed when ionic liquid and HMO were simultaneously integrated into the polymer matrix. The BCA-IL-HMO membrane exhibited the lowest strength of 18 ± 9.1 MPa, while the CA-IL-HMO demonstrated a strength of 37 ± 24.2 MPa. The dual presence of plasticizer (IL) and inorganic filler (HMO) disrupts polymer–polymer interactions, hence lowering the TS, making those membranes more fragile and potentially challenging to handle if scaled up.

content: 3.4. Membranes ion exchange capacity (IEC) The ion exchange capacity varies considerably with the membranes composition (Table 2). Among the tested membranes, the plant-based cellulose acetate (CA) membrane demonstrated the lowest ion ex­change capacity (IEC) of 0.026 ± 0.0013 mmol/g. Comparatively, the bacterial cellulose acetate (BCA) membrane displayed a higher IEC of 0.035 ± 0.0021 mmol/g. This can be attributed to the lower degree of substitution (DS) and acetylation in BCA (DS = 1.97, acetylation = 34.4 %) relative to CA (DS = 2.25, acetylation = 39.7 %). A reduced degree of substitution often leads to an increased availability of free hydroxyl groups for interaction, thereby enhancing the ion exchange capacity [57]. The main purpose of acetylation of BC was to enhance its solubility and processability. Pristine BC is highly crystalline and insoluble in most solvents. Additionally, excessive water absorption can weaken its me­chanical strength. Thus, acetylation improves solubility and balances hydrophilicity, resulting in better performance in wet applications. Therefore, acetylation is not intended to increase ion-exchange capacity, but it is necessary to impart specific mechanical and chemical properties to bacterial cellulose for targeted uses. The addition of 5 wt% ionic liquid (IL) enhanced the ion exchange capacity (IEC) for both membrane types.

The CA-IL membrane showed an IEC of 0.038 ± 0.0017 mmol/g, while BCA-IL had a slightly higher value of 0.041 ± 0.0032 mmol/g. This small increase suggests that the IL created new ionic regions or established microenvironments that boosted the mobility and accessibility of exchange sites within the membrane. Additionally, the ionic liquid may facilitate ion transport by loosening or disrupting the dense polymer structure, thus exposing more functional groups for exchange. The addition of 20 wt% of HMO in both material types (CA and BCA) has resulted in a significant increase in IEC. The CA-HMO and BCA-HMO membranes showed IEC values of 0.161 ±0.0096 mmol/g and 0.208 ± 0.0010 mmol/g, respectively. The domi­nant factor contributing to the enhanced IEC performance can be attributed to HMO’s ability to exchange protons with lithium ions through specific binding sites. The membrane using BCA as a polymer consistently outperformed those using CA, likely due to the inherent variations in microstructure and functional groups present in the two cellulose acetate variants. The combination of HMO and IL in the membrane composition demonstrated a synergistic effect on IEC values, with the highest IEC values observed for CA-IL-HMO (0.1663 ± 0.0083 mmol/g) and BCA-IL-HMO membranes (0.215 ± 0.0107 mmol/g). 3.5.

Membranes’ water uptake (%) and hydrophilicity Water uptake percentages and contact angles of all the synthesised membranes are reported in Table 3. The membranes with BCA consis­tently absorbed more water, exhibiting higher hydrophilicity (lower water contact angles), than those with CA. The pristine BCA had the highest water uptake of 31.64 ± 0.13 % and the lowest water contact angle (47.27 ± 2.82◦). This result is likely due to a reduced number of acetylation substitution groups, resulting in increased availability of free hydroxyl groups that can interact with water. The addition of ionic liquid (IL) resulted in reduced water absorption and hydrophilicity for both CA and BCA membranes. This suggests that the hydrophobic characteristics of the integrated ionic liquid [58] reduced the mem­brane’s hydrophilicity. The addition of HMO into the membrane’s ma­trix had a less noticeable effect, resulting in a slight reduction in water uptake along with moderately higher contact angles in both materials. This is probably due to a balance between HMO’s surface hydroxyl groups, which can retain water, and its filler-like behavior that limits chain mobility.

The observed differences in LSV slopes are mainly due to (i) ion mobility within the membrane phase and (ii) partitioning or sorption at the membrane-solution interface. The lithium-ideal selectivity, defined as the ratio of ionic conductivity of the membranes when immersed in 0.25 M Li2SO4 solution to that in 0.25 M MnSO4 solution for Li/Mn, and similarly for Li/Ni and Li/Co, is shown in Fig. 5(c–d).

Additionally, the incorporation of PP13–TFSI ionic liquid (IL) plasticises the synthesised membranes, as it contains a bulky, charge-delocalised TFSI-anion (e.g., PP13–TFSI), which decreases crystallinity and enlarges the fractional free volume in polar polymers, thereby enhancing ion mobility [54,59,60]. Adding inorganic lithium-selective filler (HMO) can create interfacial, less-ordered polymer regions with increased mobility, forming hydrated nanochannels that promote ion transport. This effect boosted ionic conduction for both lithium and other cations but decreased Li-ideal selectivity. In contrast, the CA-based membrane exhibits the highest Li-ideal selectivity, especially with HMO. Among CA-based membranes, CAHMO achieves the highest Li+/Ni2+ ≈112, Li+/Co2+ ≈87.7, and Li+/ Mn2+ ≈12.1 selectivity values (Fig. 5(d)). In comparison, the BCA family shows Li-ideal selectivities (e.g., BCA-IL: Li+/Ni2+ ≈18.6, Li+/ Co2+ ≈16.0, Li+/Mn2+ ≈6.9). The superior Li-ideal selectivity of CA membranes can be attributed to their higher crystalline regions and lower water uptake and ion-exchange capacity.

Additionally, their denser chain packing and reduced free volume limit nonspecific trans­port and favour the passage of smaller cations (Li+) over larger hydrated divalent ions (Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+) (Table S4 in Supporting Information). The HMO protonated spinel (H1.33Mn1.67O4), with vacant spinel sites, permits only Li+ to pass while rejecting larger divalent ions [61]. Incorporating HMO into a denser CA matrix reduces leakage pathways, enabling CA-HMO to exhibit remarkable Li-ideal selectivity of 112 and 87.7 for Li+/Ni2+ and Li+/Co2+, respectively. Considering these outcomes, the most suitable candidates to recover lithium from Li-ion battery leachate are BCA-IL and BCA-HMO. BCA-IL exhibited both relatively high Li+ conductivity with the highest BCA based membranes selectivity (Li+/Ni2+ 18.6, Li+/Co2+ 16.0, Li+/Mn2+ 6.9), while BCA-HMO membrane showed the highest Li-ion conduction (Li+ 1.0 * 10-2 S cm-1) with Li-selectivity of 10 for Li+/Ni2+, 7.7 for Li+/Co2+ and 5.6 for Li+/Mn2+. 3.7. Conversely, the BCA-HMO membrane showed lower separation factors: 3.39 (Li+/Mn2+), 3.62 (Li+/Ni2+), and 3.36 (Li+/Co2+) after 5 h (Fig. 6(b)). This data demonstrates a notable performance difference: BCA-IL maintained 3 to 4 times higher preference for Li+ transport compared to divalent cations. It is also worth noting that the separation factors of both membranes, when using complex multi-ionic feed, decreased slightly compared to the binary salt systems after 5 h of testing. For example, Li+/Mn2+ separation factor was 13.0 (BCA-IL) and 3.4 (BCAHMO) in the binary salt system, which decreased to 10.5 and 3.3, respectively, when leachate was used as feed. This expected reduction aligns with previous observations of decreased separation factor when using a binary salt mixture as feed, compared to single salt permeation in Li-ideal selectivity. The decline is attributed to increased ionic in­teractions and competitive transport, where multivalent cations begin to compete more actively for transport pathways through the membrane over time.

content: ­teractions and competitive transport, where multivalent cations begin to compete more actively for transport pathways through the membrane over time. The molar Li+ ionic flux for BCA-HMO was 0.50 mol m-2 h-1 after 5 h (Fig. 6(d)), exceeding the lithium flux (0.12 mol m-2 h-1) for BCA-IL (Fig. 6(c)). This behaviour reflects previous findings with binary feeds, where BCA-HMO attains superior ionic flux at the expense of selectivity. However, BCA-IL more effectively restricts bivalent cations but results in a lower Li+ transport rate. These findings suggest that membrane structure and chemistry, rather than ion properties alone, determine long-term transport performance when handling complex feeds. From a physicochemical perspective, Li+ consistently dominates transport due to its smaller hydrated radius (~3.82 Å) and moderate hydration enthalpy (~–520 kJ/mol), making it more mobile and more susceptible to partial dehydration at the membrane interface compared to divalent ions such as Mn2+, Ni2+, and Co2+, whose hydration energies (~–1850 to -2100 kJ/mol) and hydrated radii (~4.0–4.4 Å) pose greater barriers to partitioning into the membrane phase [62]. In BCA-IL, these intrinsic ion properties are enhanced by the PP13–TFSI ionic liquid, which likely forms a highly polar microenvironment that favours Li+ over larger, more extensively hydrated ions.

content: hanced by the PP13–TFSI ionic liquid, which likely forms a highly polar microenvironment that favours Li+ over larger, more extensively hydrated ions. PP13–TFSI ionic liquid has been documented in the literature as a lithium-selective ionic liquid used for the selective extraction of lithium from seawater [63,64]. In the BCA-HMO membrane, the ion sieving capability of hydrogen manganese oxide (H1.33Mn1.67O4), recognized as a lithium-selective filler, seems to be diminished under multicomponent feed conditions [39]. This is likely due to competition between cations for interfacial sites and less effective filler–matrix connectivity, which possibly allowed some Mn2+, Ni2+, and Co2+ to bypass the sieving function via polymer-dominated path­ways. Additionally, the high flux values indicate a more open polymer matrix in BCA-HMO, which may enhance ion mobility at the expense of selectivity. To clarify and thoroughly understand the lithium transport mechanism, molecular dynamic simulation studies should be carried out to identify which specific effect (size sieving, particular ion interactions with acetate groups, or the preferential solvation environments formed by the ionic liquid) dominates in the selective lithium transport through the developed membranes. Overall, both membranes exhibit significant Li+ recovery potential from complex leachate compositions; BCA-IL distinctly maintains superior lithium selectivity, while BCA–HMO showed higher Li-ion flux.
